I-' bR ' The Bishop of Kilmore figures as Kilware, and the late Lord Deramore |

; g we are told, assumed an additional surname three years before his | I
i 8 ~eldest brother was born. If we turn to the portion entitled, * Ulster |

[: : Homes *’ we find that it betrays an ignorance of the geography of the .
{0 province equally lamentable. We are informed twice over that Clonesis '
!_ o ‘in Fermanagh, that Richhill is in Co. Antrim, that Greencastle is in Co.

Vi Donegal, and on the same page that it is in Co. Antrim ““ on the shore of

} ~ Belfast Foyle!” Preserve us!! -

it DR. JOHN GAMBLE ‘ -
i3 Ulster has forgotten one of its cleverest sons, a man who devoted | &
2;_ most of his life to the study of its people and their characteristics,

O - and whose books did much to produce in England a kindly.feeling for

t: 28 = his countrymen. Dr. Gamble was born. in the town of Strabane; Co.

GEN e ‘Tyrone, in the early seventies of the 18th century He . recewed 8. oAy
: 4= = medical education at Edinburgh University, where he graduated. in

Y eeae 1793. Becoming a surgeon in the army, he served with Sir Ralph.

: - Abercrombie’s expedition {to Holland in 1799. An affection of. the

: - eyes soon compelled him to resign, and he returned to his native town :
-‘ 2 to devote himself to literary work as well ‘as his eyesight allowed. . 3
1 i Hemade frequent journeys.on foot, or.by coach, through the country,

Ve B o chatting with everyone he met, picking up story and legend and ‘jest, Y
. £--= - = and noting- mcldents and charactenstms. He died auddenly, whem Ligsan
1 :fj S . attending a iuneral aerv:ce, on 18th May, 1881.
